query: factors influencing maternal mortality during labor

retrieved_docs.title: Ophthalmia Neonatorum -- Prognosis -- Factors Influencing Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Several key factors influence the prognosis of ophthalmia neonatorum, each carrying significant weight in determining clinical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Antepartum Infections -- Prognosis -- Maternal Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal prognosis following antenatal infections varies widely, influenced by the type of infection, its severity, and the timing during pregnancy. While many infections are mild and manageable, some can lead to serious complications. For instance, infections like influenza can increase the risk of pneumonia and hospitalization, especially in the second trimester, and may lead to preterm labor or even maternal death in severe cases. Chorioamnionitis is associated with risks such as preterm birth, postpartum complications, hemorrhage, and sepsis. Severe infections during pregnancy are linked to higher odds of complications, with sepsis being a leading cause of maternal mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiology Morbidity And Mortality -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Morbidity and mortality are two types of retrospective information that allows for continuous evaluation of the efficacy of either a specific health care system or an implemented intervention in place. For example, the use of maternal morbidity and mortality to gauge the risks of pregnancy and childbirth, as well as the efficacy of the health care they receive, are of vital importance. [2] In a related vein, accurate assessment of these measures are crucial to understanding and evaluating their impact and trajectory. Ultimately, mortality alongside morbidity allows epidemiologists to study further the burdens that a health event may place on a population. These metrics also allow stakeholders to more effectively prioritize which health events to tackle and allocate resources toward and proactively manage the potential onset of a health event.

retrieved_docs.title: Normal Labor: Physiology, Evaluation, and Management -- Pathophysiology -- Second stage of labor
retrieved_docs.content: Based on Zhang's results, the ACOG recommends defining a prolonged 2nd stage of labor as over 3 hr of pushing in nulliparous patients and beyond 2 hr of pushing in multiparous patients. However, the ACOG notes that diagnosing arrest should be individualized, considering patient preferences and clinical factors that may affect the likelihood of vaginal delivery. These factors include fetal size and position, maternal pelvis shape, the magnitude of expulsive efforts, comorbidities such as hypertension or diabetes, age, and history of previous deliveries. [15] Second-stage arrest may also be diagnosed before these thresholds are reached if fetal rotation or descent does not occur despite adequate contractions, pushing effort, and time.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiology Morbidity And Mortality -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Interventions are also monitored during implementation and evaluated for efficacy, efficiency, impact, cost-effectiveness, and potential for improvement. Two important outcome measures are morbidity and mortality. Changes within these two measures can indicate not only the severity of a health event but also serve as one of the litmus tests for the responses that epidemiologists may take. Morbidity and mortality measures can be gathered using either descriptive or analytic epidemiology and can undergo stratification into various subcategories, such as perinatal, neonatal, infant, and maternal morbidity mortalities, to name a few. Morbidity and mortality can also be stratified by age, race,  ethnicity, sex, gender, nationality, and socioeconomic status, which provide an opportunity to uncover group-specific susceptibilities or exposures within a population.

retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Labor -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Labor is a dynamic process, and the care team's decision may change depending on maternal and fetal factors. Including patients in their treatment planning is mandatory. Collaboration, shared decision-making, and communication are all vital elements for a good outcome. Psychological and emotional support should be provided to patients experiencing labor abnormalities, as these women are at a higher risk of being diagnosed with postpartum depression.

retrieved_docs.title: Induction of Labor -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: IOL represents an opportunity to intervene in an ongoing pregnancy to influence delivery timing for maternal or fetal benefit. There is rigorous research that has been published and continues to be published on both the maternal and fetal safety of IOL, especially as it relates to cesarean section rates. Ultimately, further top-quality research evidence should focus on neonate and maternal satisfaction safety aspects. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Antepartum Infections -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Worldwide, sepsis is a leading cause of maternal morbidity and mortality, as there is frequently a delay in diagnosis and suboptimal medical management. [8] To address this issue, the WHO launched the Global Maternal Sepsis and Neonatal Initiative in collaboration with its partners in 2016. This initiative led to the implementation of the GLOSS (Global Maternal Sepsis Study) in 2017, aiming to reduce the burden of maternal sepsis and related infections. While some progress has been made in alleviating the impact of maternal sepsis and maternal infections (MSMI) across 131 LMICs, the disease burden remains significantly higher in low-income countries compared to lower-middle- and upper-middle-income countries. Factors such as socioeconomic status and access to universal health coverage play a crucial role in shaping the severity of MSMI in these regions. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: PPH is frequently subdivided based on symptom onset. Primary PPH is hemorrhage that occurs between the third stage of labor (ie, delivery of the placenta) and 24 hours after fetal delivery; secondary PPH occurs more than 24 hours after delivery—up to 12 weeks postpartum. Causes, summarized by the 4 "T's" (tone, trauma, tissue, thrombin), require prompt intervention. Management involves a multidisciplinary approach, emphasizing blood loss assessment, fluid replacement, and source control. Despite advancements, PPH prevention and prediction remain essential to maternal well-being. PPH poses a significant challenge in obstetrics, complicated by difficulties in accurately estimating blood loss, as cognitive biases can lead to delays in diagnosis and management. Though more accurate, quantitative blood loss measurement methods have not consistently improved clinical outcomes. Interprofessional approaches, including PPH bundles and perinatal quality collaboratives, reduce morbidity. Management involves a coordinated effort addressing uterine atony, genital tract lacerations, retained placental tissue, and coagulopathy, with surgical interventions like hysterectomy as a last resort. Prevention strategies include active management of the third stage of labor and prenatal identification of high-risk factors. Vigilance and prompt intervention remain crucial in mitigating maternal morbidity and mortality associated with PPH. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Labor -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and neonatal morbidity is associated with a prolonged first stage of labor, including maternal fever from endometritis, shoulder dystocia, hemorrhage, and blood transfusion. [27] [28] With an abnormal second stage of labor, oxytocin may be utilized again. An increase in third- and fourth-degree perineal lacerations, cesarean delivery, and a prolonged hospital stay is associated with a protracted or arrested second stage of labor. [29] [28] The fetus has an increased risk of NICU admission, low Apgar scores, and a need for assisted ventilation with abnormal labor. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Normal Labor: Physiology, Evaluation, and Management -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Each labor is unique, but an interprofessional approach throughout all 3 stages of labor can significantly improve patient outcomes. A Canadian retrospective cohort study of 1,238 women found that an interprofessional team approach to obstetrical care resulted in better outcomes by decreasing the rate of cesarian births and length of hospital stays for women. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Peripartum Cardiomyopathy -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: In the United States, Black women are twice as likely as White women to experience prolonged cardiac dysfunction; when recovery occurs, it takes twice as long. Mortality rates can reach 20%, with the highest rates observed among Black women in the United States and women in less developed regions. The condition can profoundly impact affected individuals, their families, and newborns during a critical life stage.

retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Labor (Nursing) -- Pearls and Other issues
retrieved_docs.content: With abnormal labor, cesarean delivery can be a life-saving procedure and may be medically necessary. Diligent management of labor aspires to minimize variation between providers as they resort to cesarean delivery for the management of abnormal labor. The new labor-management guidelines published in 2014 aim to decrease cesarean delivery rates and provide the best opportunity to improve outcomes and reduce costs as well as reduce future maternal obstetrical morbidity and mortality. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Perimortem Cesarean Delivery -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The outcomes for both mother and baby can vary, but performing a PMCD at 20 weeks or later in pregnancy may enhance survival when resuscitation efforts are unsuccessful. In cases where left uterine displacement fails to relieve compression, PMCD becomes necessary as the procedure helps both by aiding resuscitation and reducing the risk of hypoxic fetal brain damage. The decision to proceed with PMCD is complex and influenced by factors, including the underlying cause of cardiac arrest, the stage of pregnancy, and available medical resources. As a result, standardized guidelines for PMCD are limited, though the recommendations issued by the AHA on cardiopulmonary resuscitation for pregnant patients, including guidelines for PMCD, have been widely recognized. [3] [4] [1]

retrieved_docs.content: Advanced maternal age Bandl ring Chorioamnionitis Epidural analgesia Gestational diabetes Hypertensive disorders Inadequate uterine contractions Lack of supportive care during labor Large weight gain in pregnancy Non-gynecoid maternal pelvimetry Nulliparity Poor hydration and nutrition during labor Post-term pregnancy Short stature

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia (Nursing) -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Although eclampsia is uncommon in developed countries, it is still a major cause of maternal morbidity and mortality worldwide. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 734
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is a broad category of maternal complications that encompasses several specific conditions. It has as child entities postpartum hemorrhage (نزف ما بعد الولادة), failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض), and fetal death (موت الجنين). In addition, this category is classified as obstetric cervical tear (تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم) and pelvic obstetric hematoma (ورم دموي توليدي حوضي). Together, these interrelated complications illustrate the diverse and serious events that can arise during the postpartum period and influence the clinical management of the mother.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1622
retrieved_docs.content: The set of obstetric complications and risk factors grouped under the SMQ “مضاعفات وعوامل خطر الحمل والمخاض والولادة (باستثناء حالات الإجهاض وولادة جنين ميت)” includes a range of specific conditions. Twin pregnancy that results in delivery (“حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة”) is classified within this SMQ, reflecting the increased maternal and fetal risks associated with multiple gestations. Cervical dystocia (“عسر ولادة متعلق بعنق الرحم”) is also a member, indicating difficulty in cervical dilation during labor. Prolonged labor (“مخاض طويل”) and stimulated labor (“مخاض محفز”) are each identified as distinct labor abnormalities that fall under the same SMQ, highlighting their potential to contribute to adverse outcomes. Retention of the placenta or membranes without hemorrhage (“انحباس المشيمة أو الأغشية، من دون نزف”) is included, emphasizing the importance of proper placental delivery to prevent infection or other complications. Uterine atony (“ونى الرحم”) is recognized as another component of the SMQ, representing the failure of the uterus to contract effectively after delivery, which can lead to postpartum hemorrhage. Additionally, the seated delivery position without mention of rotation (“وضعية المَجيء المقعدي من دون ذكر الانقلاب”) is incorporated into the SMQ, reflecting how certain maternal positions during birth can influence labor dynamics and risk profiles. Collectively, these entities illustrate the breadth of conditions that are grouped under the SMQ for complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor, and delivery, providing clinicians with a structured framework for identifying and managing obstetric risks.

retrieved_docs.content: and abnormal forces of labor, illustrating how fetal factors can influence maternal care. Overall, the framework links broad diagnostic categories to precise maternal and fetal conditions, supporting accurate coding, clinical awareness, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition, disproportion or other labor complications are recognized as a form of failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض) and are also classified as causes of a prolonged first stage of labor (مرحلة أولى مطولة من المخاض) as well as a prolonged second stage of labor (مرحلة ثانية مطولة من المخاض). These classifications reflect that abnormal fetal positioning, size mismatch, or other intrapartum problems can impede the initiation of effective uterine contractions and can extend both early and later phases of the birthing process, highlighting the need for careful monitoring and appropriate obstetric interventions to prevent labor dystocia and improve maternal‑fetal outcomes.

These multiple‑gestation entities, together with “قلة السائل الأمنيوسي” (oligohydramnios), “تعسر المخاض” (difficult labor), “عسر ولادة متعلق بعنق الرحم” (cervical dystocia), “مخاض طويل” (prolonged labor), and “نواتج متبقية من الحمل” (outcomes of pregnancy), are classified as members of the SMQ, highlighting their role as obstetric complications or risk factors that influence maternal‑fetal outcomes. Additional conditions grouped in the same SMQ include “مخاض محفز” (stimulated labor), “إنتان الدم بعد الولادة” (post‑partum sepsis), “انحباس المشيمة أو الأغشية دون نزف” (retained placenta or membranes without bleeding), “انصمام بالسائل السلوى” (amniotic‑fluid embolism), “حمل بطني” (abdominal pregnancy), “حمل مطول” (prolonged pregnancy), “رضح توليدي آخر غير ذلك” (other types of delivery‑related postpartum hemorrhage), “مشيمة ملتصقة” (placenta accreta), “ورم

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 126
retrieved_docs.content: of mother” is classified as the delayed effect of pregnancy, birth, and puerperium complications (التأثير المتأخر لمضاعفات الحمل والولادة والنفاس). A related category, “Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother,” encompasses pelvic vascular tumor of gestational origin (ورم دموي توليدي حوضي) and is linked to fetal death (موت الجنين). Collectively, these relationships illustrate how uterine functional disorders, pelvic bony and soft‑tissue abnormalities, fetal‑pelvic disproportion, labor‑induction failures, prolonged labor stages, and anesthesia‑related maternal complications interconnect within obstetric and puerperal care, underscoring the need for precise identification of each entity to guide comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1087
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is a category of obstetric problems that includes the specific conditions فشل تحريض المخاض and موت الجنين, and this category is classified as ورم دموي توليدي حوضي. This relationship indicates that the broader group of birth‑related and puerperal complications that influence maternal management encompasses labor‑induction failure and fetal death, and that these complications are associated with, or fall under, the entity ورم دموي توليدي حوضي. Recognizing these links aids clinicians in understanding how particular adverse outcomes and underlying pelvic vascular lesions may be interconnected within the spectrum of maternal and perinatal care.

Together, these entities and relationships illustrate how specific amniotic cavity problems, early membrane rupture, cesarean delivery, missed abortion, anesthesia‑related cardiac and central nervous system complications, fetal disorders, and termination‑related hemorrhagic conditions are interlinked with pregnancy complications and their late effects, ultimately influencing the risk of delivering a non‑viable infant and underscoring the importance of careful obstetric monitoring and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical insufficiency, postpartum sepsis, retained placenta or membranes without hemorrhage, prolonged pregnancy, other previous delivery, unspecified early‑pregnancy bleeding, and malignant tumor of the placenta are all classified as members of the “Complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor and delivery (excluding miscarriage and stillbirth) (SMQ).” This SMQ category groups together a range of obstetric conditions that represent significant complications or risk factors during pregnancy, labor, and the postpartum period, emphasizing their importance for clinical assessment, monitoring, and management in maternal‑fetal care.

retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery, delivered, with mention of postpartum complication and Central nervous system complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery, delivered, with mention of postpartum complication are both classified as Other and unspecified complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother. This broader category of Other and unspecified complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother also includes postpartum hemorrhage (نزف ما بعد الولادة), fetal death (موت الجنين), a birth‑related cervical tear (تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم), the delayed effect of pregnancy, birth and postpartum complications (التأثير المتأخر لمضاعفات الحمل والولادة والنفاس), and a pelvic birth‑related vascular tumor (ورم دموي توليدي حوضي). retrieved_docs.content: in labor and delivery (with postpartum mention) and central nervous system complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery (with postpartum mention). Both anesthesia‑related cardiac and CNS complications are classified under “Other and unspecified complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother,” highlighting their impact on maternal care after delivery, and they are identified as narrower subsets of pregnancy complications, emphasizing that they arise in the context of gestation and delivery. Fetal disorder (“اضطراب جنيني”) is recorded as a parent of pregnancy complications, underscoring the contribution of intrinsic fetal abnormalities to maternal obstetric risk. The late‑effect category “التأثير المتأخر لمضاعفات الحمل والولادة والنفاس” (late effect of pregnancy, birth, and postpartum complications) is classified as a type of pregnancy complication, linking long‑term sequelae to the acute obstetric event.
